SAMUDL McSKIMMIN. HISTORIAN 0F CARRICKTERGUS . Br THOMAS  DAVIS. In the obituary of last month we see the name of Samuel McSkim- min,  or 0 Sam McSkimmin, as he was called and called himself. His ancestor four generations  before him WDS 4 miller of Lurgan in 1641. McSkimmin used to say this man had come from England with the Brownlows,  but how an Englishman got the name of McSkimmin he culd not htell accout for. Sam McSkimmin himself was born at Carrickfergus some time in 1775, and died on l7th Febrwary, 1843. He was a middle-sized man, with small, ivegular features, dark com - plexion ,   keen, anxious look, uassuming, eager and business-like manners . He taught himself to wlite. He used to cut off the tops from the  old copy  hooks of a school in his neighbourhood, and never had any instruction but what he procured  for himself: His position was equally discouraging ; he kept a small shop for groceries, candles, provisions, etc., in 2 back street in Carrickfergus and his library study, museum, and, if we recollect rightly , his bedroom, too, were al1 in one little room off his shop. Yet he never was   ashamed of this He minded his business,  apparently both because he liked it and be_ cause it was his only way of getting books. His books rere few, but of a good kind and well used. Doubtless his   enjoyment  and us0 of them were the greater  from the pains he was at in procuring them Yet .this man, s0 brought up and s0   circumstanced, was a contributor to the best  magazines of the day, and the mwiter of one of the best local histories we have. His  acquirements were  chiefly 01 exclusivelz Irish history and antiquities and zoology. Amongst his  contributions to the magazines were papers on Ex- tinct Birds )1 The Irish Round Towers 1) and on in the Gentleman' $ Magazine The Secret History of the Insurrection 0f 1803 ' in ; Fraser, in which there were some curioug details, mixed up with  the ebsurd  gogsip of the  government  party  against the insurgents and' with bitterness   against the United Irishmea. mapked   throughout

THI IRISH BOOK LOVIR.
86
From whatever cause he hated them   cordially ; aud had hc  lived t complete the (( History  of the United Irishmen 1 ) on which he was engaged and  for which he had sone Orange MS. 7 the value   of which he greatly over-rated, it would have  been almost a8 acrimonious and unjust a,S Musgrave. He thoughit them all rogues and   villaing. Any evidence Fas good enough against them, and this not from a want of principle, by any means, but from mere prejudice: He printed in the United   Service Journal Recollections of the Peninsular War;" Tbis he had taken down from the recital of an old blind pengioner who had served in that FaT . The  University Magazine, too, if FC mistake not, contained some articles of his: But his principal  writing was the history of his native town. The frst edition WaS printed   by H. K. Gordon in Belfast; 1811, and Tas sustained by 2 subscription  list of about 440 names 1t con- tains 140 pages on the   history, topography and  antiquities of the town and neighbourhood, and 17   appendices of documents on 1to boundaries and   history, natural] and civil. This book went through three   editions, Or four, rather ; for though his last title page bears date 1832, he had   twenty-eight additional pages printed in  1833, and sewed up in it. Thus increased, it contains 430 pages with maps and plates . The first   part contains annals ofthe town,  compiled with great care from al] the Anglo-Irish histories. McSkimmin   did not know  Irish, which limited  his information on our older history to the Anti-Irish authorities. Amougst many other parts of great value these annals  contain the only full account of Thurot's invasion iu February, 1760 . The second, third and fourth parts   contain the topo- graphy, antiquities, manners  and statistics of Oarrickfergus, for which his authorities were the MS. records of the town, which seem to have been carefully kept, and the MSS. of one Henry Gill, who rase from being a  sttall keeper in rthe market to /be  Mayor of Oanrriok, and died rich and respected in  1761. His   appendix   contains a vast mas8 of minute biography and local hstory. Whenever he deals with local history, from local authoritiee he is usually just and en tertaining; but often in using general  histories he adopts all their errorg. It appears that he had obtained from a copy of the depositions a8 to Antrim from TC.D ,_ ) and he aotually regarded them &8 good authorities. Still; he wag sometimeg very fairs He ueed to prefer  the poorer Roman   Oatholics to the   Protegtants:
R

TH IRISH BOOK LOVER
87
When vou inquiro about any ruins OT remarkable places from Roman Catholic, he asks you if 2 Saint is buried there, ad tries t0 make it out with will for you The Protestant asks if money is buried there, and expects to be paid for showing sou the way . The Roman Catholics," he added, have more feeling for old times. 11 He has assured uS,  too, that   Ouruy' s account of the massacre of the Mc- Naughtens at Ballycastle by Monroe was understated, and for this he referred not only to the tradition of the place, but also to acopy of & contemporary pamphlet, written out and sent him by Mr: Crofton Croker. His store of traditions was great he used to go walking aboub the Cbunty Antrim  gossipping with the people. The only way, in his Fere up 1) opinion, to come at the truth from the poorer people who in '98 mas to make them free with drink, and i. this 'Way he said he had laid cut a good deal of money for his information His traditions Fere chiefy clustered about '98, and the 0'Neals, from Shane 'to Owen Roe. The   formerv he said, was   killed about three miles north of Oushendun, in the camp of Alexander Oge MDonnell, and that valu- ablo traditions of Shane were to /e met amonsgt the Roman Catholio peasantry . It is to be hoped that the manuscripts of tlus   patient, learned, and original man will be taken care of and  published, The Nation, 4th March, 1843.
IRISH SCHOUARSHIP AT LOUVAIN. Among the clleges of fhe University of Louvain were three founded by Irichmnen for the benefit of Irish soholars coming  abroad for educe- tion _those of the Dominioans, the Franciscans ad the Pastoral Col- lege Of these the most  illustrious was  the cele- ifor   secular clergy. St.Anthony of  Padua, illustrious hot brated   Firanciscan   College of only as compared with its brother colleges at Louvain but in compari- at home or abroad , It Fag the son with  all other Irish icolleges eyes looked wtil   affection and hope as 2 plaoe centre to which  Trish of shelter: in trouibled and penilous peviod, a8 2 place of learning in a whioh their poiers ad aspirations might fnd training and encourage scholars worked, whose labourg ment. Wiithin its walls i: group of bave added Justre to the annals of iheir counthy. There Ward designed the to completion great compilations   whioh were   afternvards biought Thomas Serin,  Stephen by Jobn Colgan ; there  Patrick   Fleming and White discussed  how best   their country' 8 menl- and Luke Wad ling,

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.
88
orials  might be utilized and preserved . From its   shelter, Peter Iom - bard , Hugh  McOaghwell and other prelates passed backwards and for- wards to Treland 0n1 missions partly religious, partly politioal, Here, too, Bonaventure 0'Hosey 'wrote his metrical catechism in Irish, and composed his qoems. We have to-day in the British  Museum one of the rare copies of ihis early printed book, Teagasg Criosdaidhe;' and indeed a very interesting collection of the early  printed books from Louvain  dating from 1608 onward. For at Louvain was established the   first   foreign iprinting  press which had 2 fount of Irish type, &nd long before any Iooks, except the New Testament and Book   of Com- non Prayer , had been printed in Irish in Ireland itself. 0'Clery and Colgan' s works containing extraots in Irish and little  religious   books in Irish and Latin were issued from the presses there. It is not clear whether the Irigh type was out albroad 01 transferred from Ireland . It is known that some Irish type 'which Queen Elizabeth caused to be struck   in Irelaad for' the purpose of printing  the New  Testament and thc Book of Common  Prayer,  disappeared   from that   counbry in the same yead , and no more Irish Fas printed in Ireland until 1687, when new typpe had to bbe made to pint Bishop Bedell'$ Bible The foreign Irish colleges were usually  founded hy Irish merohants settled  abroad, 01 Irish officers  in the service of foreign princes, and sometimes Iby  Sovereigus. Had it not been for these colleges the Religious Orders   might have died olt in Ireland during the I6th and 17th centuies. Up to the time of the (6 Flight  of dhe Dan-ls 9) there seem to have bbeen in Ulsiter"   alone sixteen  monasteries remaining, of w-hich the famous and beautiful monastery of  Donegal Fas the chief: It is perhaps owing to the faot that the religious houses existed longer in the North that so large ia proportion of the most prominent Fran- ciscans of Louvain were Ulsteamen. McCaghwell,   0'Clery , Cblgan, Ward,  Fleming wiere all Ulstermen. It is to the History of the Irish Tranciscan  Houses; 9} wwritten in Louvain in 1617 by Donatus Mooney, the Provincial, ergtwhile & trooper'  of 0'Neill'$, that we are indelbted for nearly   adl 'we know of these once flourishhing houses 80 rapidly being destroyed in  lhis lifetime Father   Meehhan made good use of thhiis manusoript in  compiling his Histor of the Irish Fran- ciscan  Monasteries; 9) but the original  document  still  lies  unpublished in the Bibliotheque   Royal ait; Brussels, 4, cOpY , possibly Wadding's, also being found in the Franciscan Monastery, Dublin, transferred

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.
89
thither' , with many other documents of importance, fqom the library of Louvain. Undowbtedly to Irish   people the most notable event in the history of Louvain i8 the visit t0 the city of the exiled earls and tbeir families  and followers iu 1607-8, of whose adventues a narnative still exists written at that time by one of the party of tho Aight;"' Teigue 0'Keenan, the distorian of the Maguires: 0'Keenan' s accout of the Flight of the  Earls, 1 ) in his own handwriting, w38 long deposited, among other documents,  in the Frnciscan Convent at Lourain  and is QOW at the Franoiscan  Monastery, Merchant' s Quay, Dublin. Amongst  this colleotion is also to be found the original MS . of the Ossianic poems which were sent  from  Donegal to Capt.  Sorley MaDonald in 1626-27 , edited  for the Irish Texts   Society in 1908 by John   McNeill, whilst   another volume  published by the sane society , The Adventures of Suibhne Geilt; 0 ) edited  by J. G. 0'Keefe, makes use of a  Brusselg MS . written by the hand of 0'Olery. ELLANOR HOLL:

THI STORY OF MARSHS  LIBRARY . This famous Julding that stands beneath the shade of St. Patrick's Oathedral iwas commenced about the year   1700 and completed about 1707 , The stately oak bookcases and fittings we unchanged since those dim, dar-off days of the   carly cighteenth   cntury, when many notable figures  in Irsh  history must have marched along the time- worn corridors and handled thbe 1re tomes of the first  public library in Dublin. The great Dean (Swift) himself was among the frst Governors, and attended a visitation in 1718. His last recorded attendance mas in 1736. We have many memoriies of him here, especi- ally in the volimes of Clarendon' s History , which he freely covered with  lead  pencil notes  indicative of his hatred of the Scotch nation In later years Tom Moore used the   library   with advantage and has written about it in  preface to some of his poems: For the mean6 of collecting the materials of the notes appended to the Translation (of Anacreon) I 'Fas chiefly   indebted to the old library adjoining St. Patrick' s Cathedral. Through my acquaintance with the deputy librarian , the Rev Di.  Cradock, I enjoyed the   privilege of constant access to this collection even at that  period of the year Fhen it is almays closed to the public. On: these occasions I used to be locked in there alone ; and to the many   solitary   hours, which I passed  in huting through the dusty  tomes of this   old   library, I owe much of that odd and out-of-the-way sot of reading which may be found scat- tered  through some of my earlier   writings ' Having completed the first portion  of the library   building Arch- bishop Marsh proceeded to stock it by purchasing in 1704 the entire librar 0f Stillingfleet, Bishop of Worcester, who had died in 1699. These tomes   numbered 9,512, and 'Fere purchased for  82,500. They had been described in England as the mine whence Richard   Bentley drew the main stores of his learning; and there was 2 good deal of indignation when the library Fa8 transferred to Ireland. Eliag Bouhereau was the next  benefactor; for he added, his own library of 2,078 volumes to Marsh'8. This man was the first librarian and wag a French Hugenot who had fed from France after the Revo cation of the Edict of Nantes. The nert most important  donations were Marsh's own books (all  marked on the dy-leaf with the motto, 'Pantache Ten Aletheian;") and the bequest in 1745 by John Stearne, Bishop of 9,000 books. To-day the   libraiy i8 in of  Clogher; some

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.
92
possession of somo 25,000 volumes  In tho early days sonio 0f the books wore aatually chained, 1) and the visitor is   still shown the Io- maing of tho locks and sockets and the wooden bars over which the ringg Fero passed. In  'spite of theso  carly  precautions many of the smaller volumes   (which were never chained) disappeared from the library, and Mp, indeed, to 1828 somo 1,200 volumes wero stolen In 1738 the librarian complained that 2 great number of bookg Gere rery lately stolen out of the library, and that many other books Fero abused and rendered imperfect by having whole tracts, maps, piotures, eto:, torn out of them Again, in 1747, the same complaint i8 1 renewed, and, to remedy as much as possible this evil, and to guard against the thefts of such detestablo villains for the future, It i8 recommended to the librarian to employ an honest  Porter who may constantly attend at the door during library hours, and watch aud search every suspeoted person  that may  happen to be admitted into the library In 1750 the librarian was ordered to place a notice at the entrance, which was still there in 1818, to the following efeot A considerable number of books   having ibeen, from time to time, stolen from the library by persons under the denomination of gentle men, claiming 9 privilege to read in it, according to the statutes, to guard against the thefts of such infamous villaing in future, no person will be admitted, unless  he produce a well-attested   certificate to the librarian of his being a scholan and a gentleman. An honest porter is watch and search every person leaving the library.' 1) appointed to As our  present Librarian, the Rev. Newport J. D. White, DD,, remarks in his admirable paper on Marsh'$ in the Library Assooiation (( honest porter W Record for Maroh; 1899, it is to be feared that this bad no objective existence, for the same noan is heard again in 1757 and 1779 As we pass into the nineteenth century we find the library pasging out of popular favour ind many of the readers under" the denomination of  gentlemen 1) taking   themselves elsewhere. The libr rary   buildings, too, wero sadly   neglected, and in 1820 they were epproaching a state of ruin. This continued until 1862 , when Sir Benjamin Lee  Guinness came formard with his generous offer of restoring St. Patrick's Oathedral, 'and included in this offer the restor- ation ialso of Margh'$ Library. In concluding this  short article I nay mention Ihat there are many ghosts connected with tho library, eepecially thet of tho Archbishop himself, who may be heard late at

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER
93
uight shifting the ladders from one side of the corridor to tho other, dusting the anoient   volumes   with  loving care, and ' always searching (acoording to my predecessor in the assistant-librarianship, Mr. J. Ra_ Scott  _who is also an authority on phantoms) for a love-letter"   which this cnfirmed old bachelor had mislaid. Of  what benefit   would it be for me to relate my on personal experiences? Would Jour  readerg believe me if I told then of that dark   evening in November , when pasging  along the  corridor towards the outer  entrance, I came gud- denly upon the great Dean himself  standing by the old oak staircase, who, pointing towards the rroom   where Clarendon's three volumes of the Rebellion are under Jock iand key, told me of his wish to erace tho load pencil remarks upon the margins as he had since discovered that ho 'Fas mistaken in the Scotch oharacter. Or of the number of times that our Librarian has set out for Trinity College, but has been held ap for hours in the passage near the door and forced to hold keen theologioal disputes wth the Archbishop on the ground   floor. If ever likely to be haunted it must   surely be Marsh's Library. a placo was Is it not w haunted house to those readers who ar0 cnpable of realis- ing that the dusty, faded covers of the anoent tomes Tiap round many the   library walls ,  and   crowded round a heart of gold, while bbeyond the old Cathedral, lie s0 many melancholy memorials of death ? Ae I write the 'winter sun is setting across the old grey tombstones that like time-warn   sentinels to mark some few   handfuls of stand there in  unremembered   graves. So to this end Te all must hushing dust come at last and leave the sunshine and the sng of birds-_love, friend- ship, strong ambition. RANDAL McDONNILL.

It is numiber had heon delivered and published. The inaugural city, such a and   Associationg that dave been   printed, a  cus - lectures at Societies innumerable. It i8 stated tomary   comipliment, must be well   nigh it  what JoU will, reached it8 that the habit, custom, system; oaHl )) of last centuy, and as 2 proof, is ' adduced climax jn the sixties

THE IRISH BOOK LOVIR:
94
the four 01" fve volumes of the Lectures to Young Men 9) (Hodges and Figgis) , and the four volumes of (( Afternoon Lectures in Litera ture and Art " (Bell and Daldy) , by some of the grentest scholars and publicists   of tlhat day . That the custom stil fourishes we hape abundant evidence, aud the  following notes 01 some recent discourse8 may merit a space in your pages. Last month Mr. J. E. Edge, K.C,, author of { An Irish   Utopia;" lectured on his Recollections  of Old Times;' 1) which include some interesting literary reminiscences. His  father, Jolu D Edge, Isaac Butt, and his brother-in-law , John Swanzy (father of Sir Henry), ad Dr . Maunsell stated a paper"  called The Protestant Guardian," of which Edge was editor: Maunsell and Charles Lever  were both mem- bers of the medical pofession, and had kindred literary tastes Ther , with Butt, did tho dbest writing in the Dublin   University  Magazine 1) in its palmy days . On the death of the elder Idge, in 1842, J, Sheri- dan Lefanu, the well-knawa novelist, iurchased ' the Guadian 9) and merged  it in  his own  paper, The Wiarder, ) afterwards editing the Evening Mail " Stephen   Nolan Elington, who succeeded Joln King as editor of ' Saunder's News Letter, ias the poed of the Home Circuit, and had an appropriate cong ready for every event. I knelr Mr . Fitzpatvick, the author of The Sham Squire,' Secret Service Under Pitt; the lives of Bishop Doyle and Charles Lever' , and mang other works. He had some of the traits of Bostell. He had au ingatiable desire to get iacourate information. People were   afraid of him Some did not wish to be quoted. Others "esented   being button- boled . His marvellous industry rescued  from oblivion and  destruotion many opinions, statements, and detters which  all aided in tho making of Irish history . His Life of Bishop Doyle putt Ibefore the public the career of that remarkable Prelate. The shortter" Life of the Bishop by Mi . Michael MacDonagh   displays more   literary   ability , and is more interesting reading, but @ does not give as many details as Mr Fitz patrick'8  larger  volume " The National Literary  Society of Dublin  inaugurated  its ner Beg- Bion by an address from its President , Dr: Sigerson; ihe gnand old man of Irish literature, on Ancient  Irish   Friendships." He dwelt  upon the early intercourse betiveen the Galls   from the Novrh;" and the I Men of the Weot;" and   dispelled many cloudg obat have   gathered around the Norse invaders. As a result of his investigations in the

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER.
95
ealiest records, he was led to assert ihat the relationship between tho poples TaS, on the whole, one of long-standing friendship. They tmo Foes  for  arhile, then   friends  for everr. The frequent intet- Tere nriages between them ; the numiber of Norse scholars who focked to schools ; the Irish   place names yet extant in Norray, and the our references to Erin and the Irish in Norse   history g0 '9, long way to rards disillusioning us about   thhe  Danes, who Tere no enemies of Ireland, but   friends and   admirers. The next lecture to this Society mas by Dr . J.P. Mahaffy, who has at last attained the objeot of his laudable ambition, the provostship of TCD. His subject was @he Pre-Celtic Population of Ireland " He claimed that the distinguishing  cllaraoteristics of Irish Art aud Music, usually described as Celtic;' " aare not really due to the Celts, hut are manifestation o the genius of an earlier   population, the Firbolgs, rhose influence is still felt in Ireland. In support of his  contention, he referred to ( Celtic 1) ornament in Treland wdich was remarkable for an elaboration of detail not to be found in the ornaments of any other; Celtic people in  Europe. Another noteworthy Jecture was that on The Symbolism of Church Architecture, delivered in St.  Patrick's College, Maynooth, on 10th December , bby Count Plunkett. As all your"   readers know , tihe Count is a many-isided man, whose reputation is not by any means confined to his own  country, foi:   several  continental   academies, in addition to ou"  OwI , claim him a8  memibber . He occupies the presidential  chair of R.S.A.I, in succession to many other;   distinguished men, and is hon- oured in the art world, not only @s a oritic of taste and discernment, but as the the standard   work on Botticelli. He has also author of letters itmess Hibernia, 9) done as whhich in good service io Irish its short career   nultured not a ffew   'wiriters known to and meteoric fame to-day . In ishort, no Imish literary movement for the past fonty years has pbeen without his genial help and encouragement, and it is gretifying to that his off-spring have inherited the   game   spirit kow and bid fadr to go faq' in the same direction. W. &. F.
